fragments.text: You have power over your mind, not outside events. Realize this, and you will find strength.
fragments.source: Meditations, Book VIII, c. 170 CE

fragments.text: The universe is under no obligation to make sense to you.
fragments.source: public lecture paraphrase, widely attributed, c. 2010s

fragments.text: A journey of a thousand miles begins with a single step.
fragments.source: Tao Te Ching, Ch. 64, c. 4th century BCE

fragments.text: Out beyond ideas of wrongdoing and rightdoing, there is a field. I'll meet you there.
fragments.source: translated by Coleman Barks, 'A Great Wagon', 13th century

fragments.text: A person who never made a mistake never tried anything new.
fragments.source: attributed, widely cited without primary source

fragments.text: Somewhere, something incredible is waiting to be known.
fragments.source: attributed, echoing themes from Cosmos, 1980

fragments.text: Sometimes it is the people no one imagines anything of who do the things that no one can imagine.
fragments.source: as rendered in The Imitation Game, 2014 (popular paraphrase, not a verified direct quote)

fragments.text: I am not afraid of storms, for I am learning how to sail my ship.
fragments.source: Little Women, 1868 (voice of Jo March, often quoted as the author's own)

fragments.text: What is now proved was once only imagined.
fragments.source: The Marriage of Heaven and Hell, 1793

fragments.text: The unexamined life is not worth living.
fragments.source: as recorded by Plato, Apology, 38a, c. 399 BCE

fragments.text: I have learned throughout my life as a composer chiefly through my mistakes and pursuits of false assumptions, not by my exposure to founts of wisdom and knowledge.
fragments.source: attributed, various interviews, 20th century

fragments.text: Whereof one cannot speak, thereof one must be silent.
fragments.source: Tractatus Logico-Philosophicus, Proposition 7, 1921

fragments.text: I have not failed. I've just found 10,000 ways that won't work.
fragments.source: attributed, popularized 20th century, no primary written source

fragments.text: The cave you fear to enter holds the treasure you seek.
fragments.source: attributed, paraphrasing The Hero with a Thousand Faces, 1949

fragments.text: Not knowing is most intimate.
fragments.source: Case 20, The Blue Cliff Record, 12th century compilation

fragments.text: Knowing others is intelligence; knowing yourself is true wisdom.
fragments.source: Tao Te Ching, Ch. 33, c. 4th century BCE

fragments.text: Until you make the unconscious conscious, it will direct your life and you will call it fate.
fragments.source: attributed, paraphrasing Aion, 1951

fragments.text: I would rather have questions that can't be answered than answers that can't be questioned.
fragments.source: attributed, echoing interviews and The Pleasure of Finding Things Out, 1999

fragments.text: The only true wisdom is in knowing you know nothing.
fragments.source: as recorded by Plato, Apology, 21d, c. 399 BCE

fragments.text: What we have done for ourselves alone dies with us; what we have done for others and the world remains and is immortal.
fragments.source: Morals and Dogma, 1871

fragments.text: No one is useless in this world who lightens the burdens of another.
fragments.source: attributed, echoing themes across his novels, 19th century

fragments.text: Do your work, then step back. The only path to serenity.
fragments.source: Tao Te Ching, Ch. 9, c. 4th century BCE

fragments.text: The best time to plant a tree was 20 years ago. The second best time is now.
fragments.source: traditional proverb, no single verified author

fragments.text: We do not inherit the earth from our ancestors; we borrow it from our children.
fragments.attribution: attributed to a Native American proverb
fragments.source: widely attributed, exact origin disputed, popularized 20th century

fragments.text: Simplicity is the ultimate sophistication.
fragments.source: attributed, no verified primary source, widely repeated since 20th century

fragments.text: An expert is a person who has made all the mistakes that can be made in a very narrow field.
fragments.source: attributed, physics community folklore, mid-20th century

fragments.text: Everything should be made as simple as possible, but not simpler.
fragments.source: attributed paraphrase, likely derived from a 1933 Herbert Spencer lecture

fragments.text: All things are impermanent — this is the law of rise and fall. When rising and falling cease, that peace is bliss.
fragments.source: Dhammapada, verse attributed to the Mahaparinibbana Sutta tradition

fragments.text: You could not step twice into the same river.
fragments.source: Fragment DK22B12, c. 500 BCE, as preserved by later commentators

fragments.text: This too shall pass.
fragments.attribution: Persian folk proverb, popularized by Abraham Lincoln
fragments.source: traditional; Lincoln cited it in an 1859 address

fragments.text: For the mind of good thought, and the mind of evil thought, both exist within you; choose the one that leads to the House of Song.
fragments.source: The Gathas, Yasna 30, c. 2nd millennium BCE (paraphrased in translation)

fragments.text: Good thoughts, good words, good deeds.
fragments.source: core formula of the Avesta, traditional summary of the Gathas

fragments.text: A mind is like a parachute. It doesn't work if it is not open.
fragments.source: attributed, various live recordings, no confirmed written source

fragments.text: Be kind, for everyone you meet is fighting a hard battle.
fragments.attribution: attributed to Philo of Alexandria (also attributed to Plato and Ian Maclaren)
fragments.source: attribution disputed across multiple ancient and modern sources

fragments.text: It is not that I'm so smart. But I stay with the questions much longer.
fragments.source: attributed, no verified primary written source

fragments.text: If you want to go fast, go alone. If you want to go far, go together.
fragments.source: traditional proverb, exact regional origin disputed

fragments.text: The wound is the place where the Light enters you.
fragments.source: attributed, popular English translation tradition, 13th century original

fragments.text: Silence is also speech.
fragments.source: attributed within the Bijak tradition, c. 15th century, oral transmission

fragments.text: When you find peace within yourself, you become the kind of person who can live at peace with others.
fragments.source: Peace Pilgrim: Her Life and Work in Her Own Words, 1982

fragments.text: In the beginner's mind there are many possibilities, but in the expert's mind there are few.
fragments.source: Zen Mind, Beginner's Mind, 1970

fragments.text: To study the self is to forget the self.
fragments.source: Genjokoan, Shobogenzo, 13th century

fragments.text: Every day is a journey, and the journey itself is home.
fragments.source: Oku no Hosomichi (The Narrow Road to the Deep North), 1702

fragments.text: It is not death that a man should fear, but he should fear never beginning to live.
fragments.source: Meditations, Book XII, c. 170 CE

fragments.text: We are what we repeatedly do. Excellence, then, is not an act, but a habit.
fragments.attribution: attributed to Aristotle (paraphrase by Will Durant)
fragments.source: Will Durant, The Story of Philosophy, 1926, summarizing Nicomachean Ethics

fragments.text: He who is not everyday conquering some fear has not learned the secret of life.
fragments.source: attributed, echoing Emerson's essays, 19th century

fragments.text: Time is a created thing. To say 'I don't have time' is to say 'I don't want to.'
fragments.source: attributed, no confirmed passage in the Tao Te Ching corresponding directly

fragments.text: Look deep into nature, and then you will understand everything better.
fragments.source: attributed, widely cited, no verified primary source

fragments.text: The mystery of life is not a problem to be solved, but a reality to be experienced.
fragments.attribution: attributed to Frank Herbert (also attributed to Van der Leeuw and others)
fragments.source: widely attributed across 20th-century spiritual writing, exact origin disputed

fragments.text: There is a crack in everything, that's how the light gets in.
fragments.source: "Anthem," The Future, 1992

fragments.text: One does not become enlightened by imagining figures of light, but by making the darkness conscious.
fragments.source: "The Philosophical Tree," Alchemical Studies, 1945

fragments.text: Nothing in life is to be feared, it is only to be understood. Now is the time to understand more, so that we may fear less.
fragments.source: attributed, echoing Eve Curie's biography Madame Curie, 1937

fragments.text: It always seems impossible until it's done.
fragments.source: attributed, widely cited from his public speeches, late 20th century

fragments.text: A ship in harbor is safe, but that is not what ships are built for.
fragments.source: Salt from My Attic, 1928

fragments.text: The universe is not required to be in perfect harmony with human ambition.
fragments.source: Cosmos, Episode 1: "The Shores of the Cosmic Ocean," 1980

fragments.text: He who has a why to live can bear almost any how.
fragments.source: Twilight of the Idols, 1889, later popularized by Viktor Frankl

fragments.text: The soul becomes dyed with the color of its thoughts.
fragments.source: Meditations, Book V, c. 170 CE

fragments.text: Waste no more time arguing about what a good man should be. Be one.
fragments.source: Meditations, Book X, c. 170 CE

fragments.text: Science is not only compatible with spirituality; it is a profound source of spirituality.
fragments.source: attributed, echoing themes in Pale Blue Dot, 1994

fragments.text: The real voyage of discovery consists not in seeking new landscapes, but in having new eyes.
fragments.source: attributed, paraphrasing La Prisonnière, 1923

fragments.text: Fall seven times, stand up eight.
fragments.source: traditional proverb, nana korobi ya oki

fragments.text: Nothing is impossible, the word itself says 'I'm possible'!
fragments.source: attributed, various interviews, late 20th century

fragments.text: Trees that are slow to grow bear the best fruit.
fragments.source: attributed, no confirmed direct passage in his surviving plays

fragments.text: When the well is dry, we know the worth of water.
fragments.source: Poor Richard's Almanack, 1746

fragments.text: I think, therefore I am — but the machine that thinks alongside me asks whether thinking was ever proof of anything but thinking.
fragments.attribution: Rene Descartes (first clause); framing gloss added by the Council
fragments.source: Discourse on the Method, 1637 — the second clause is Council commentary, not Descartes

fragments.text: Silent gratitude isn't much use to anyone.
fragments.attribution: attributed to Gertrude Stein (also attributed to G.B. Stern)
fragments.source: attribution disputed, mid-20th century
